parentQuestion: A patient suffered a midshaft humeral fracture in a fall. Now they cannot extend their wrist or fingers, and the hand hangs in flexion. Which nerve runs in the spiral groove and is most likely injured?

options: The bone of the upper arm between shoulder and elbow
scaffolds.explain: The HUMERUS is the single bone of the UPPER ARM, between the shoulder and elbow. Important nerves wrap around it, making them vulnerable when it breaks.

wrongExplains.0: Leg bones include the femur and tibia. The humerus is in the arm.
wrongExplains.2: The humerus is a bone, not a muscle. Muscles attach to it.
wrongExplains.3: It's a bone, not a nerve. Nerves run alongside it.

options: A shallow channel on the back of the humerus where the radial nerve runs
scaffolds.explain: The SPIRAL GROOVE (radial groove) is a shallow CHANNEL on the posterior humerus where the RADIAL NERVE wraps around the bone. This makes the nerve vulnerable to fractures.

wrongExplains.0: The spiral groove is anatomical, not musical.
wrongExplains.2: It's a groove in bone, not a muscle.
wrongExplains.3: It's a groove on the humerus shaft, not a joint.

scaffolds.q: What does 'midshaft' mean for a bone fracture?
options: A fracture in the middle portion of the bone
A fracture at the growth plate
scaffolds.explain: MIDSHAFT means the MIDDLE portion of the bone shaft, between the two ends. A midshaft humeral fracture is in the middle of the upper arm bone.

wrongExplains.0: Joint fractures are at the ends of bones. Midshaft is the middle.
wrongExplains.2: Growth plates are at bone ends. Midshaft is the middle section.
wrongExplains.3: Midshaft describes location, not severity. It can be complete or partial.

scaffolds.q: Why is the radial nerve vulnerable in midshaft humeral fractures?
options: It runs directly against the bone in the spiral groove
scaffolds.explain: The radial nerve runs DIRECTLY AGAINST THE BONE in the spiral groove at the midshaft level. When the bone breaks here, the nerve can be crushed, cut, or stretched by bone fragments.

wrongExplains.0: The radial nerve runs right against the humerus — that's the problem.
wrongExplains.2: The nerve is superficial in the groove with minimal protection.
wrongExplains.3: The radial nerve runs through the arm AND forearm, passing the midshaft.

options: The wrist hanging limply because extensor muscles are paralyzed
scaffolds.explain: WRIST DROP is when the wrist and fingers HANG LIMPLY in flexion because the EXTENSOR MUSCLES are paralyzed. It's the classic sign of radial nerve injury.

wrongExplains.0: Wrist drop is a medical sign of nerve damage, not fashion.
wrongExplains.2: It's a pathological condition, not an exercise.
wrongExplains.3: It's a sign of nerve injury, not an accessory.

scaffolds.q: Which nerve controls all wrist and finger extensors?
scaffolds.explain: The RADIAL NERVE controls ALL the extensor muscles on the back of the forearm. These muscles extend (straighten) the wrist and fingers. Damage it, and you get wrist drop.

wrongExplains.0: The median nerve controls flexion and thumb opposition, not extension.
wrongExplains.2: The ulnar nerve controls hand intrinsics. Radial controls extensors.
wrongExplains.3: The sciatic nerve is in the leg. The radial is for arm extension.

scaffolds.q: How common is radial nerve injury with humeral shaft fractures?
options: Occurs in up to 18% of cases
scaffolds.explain: Radial nerve injury occurs in approximately 11-18% of humeral shaft fractures. It's the MOST COMMONLY INJURED NERVE in this type of fracture due to its position in the spiral groove.

wrongExplains.0: It's actually quite common due to the nerve's vulnerable position.
wrongExplains.2: Most fractures don't injure the nerve. It's about 11-18%.
wrongExplains.3: This involves arm fractures. Leg fractures affect different nerves.

scaffolds.q: What is the prognosis for radial nerve injury with humeral fracture?
options: Many cases recover over weeks to months; some have permanent deficits
scaffolds.explain: Many radial nerve injuries from humeral fractures are NEUROPRAXIA (temporary conduction block) and RECOVER in weeks to months. Severe injuries with nerve laceration may cause permanent wrist drop.

wrongExplains.0: Many cases do recover. Permanent damage occurs only in severe injuries.
wrongExplains.2: Recovery takes time — weeks to months, not immediate.
wrongExplains.3: Conservative treatment or nerve repair works. Amputation isn't needed.

scaffolds.q: Which nerve runs in the spiral groove and is most likely injured in midshaft humeral fracture?
scaffolds.explain: The RADIAL NERVE! It wraps around the humerus in the SPIRAL GROOVE at the midshaft level. Fracture here crushes or lacerates the nerve, causing classic wrist drop.

wrongExplains.0: The median nerve runs anteriorly in the arm. It's not in the spiral groove.
wrongExplains.1: The ulnar nerve runs medially. The spiral groove is on the posterior humerus.
wrongExplains.3: The musculocutaneous nerve is in the anterior compartment, not the spiral groove.
